intro.description: The precise mechanics behind the world's steepest routes – from the Riggenbach ladder to the modern Von Roll system.

detail.stats_note: NOTE: Technical specifications are based on historical sources and documented specifications for each rack system.
detail.photo_alt: Photo of the {{system}} system with highlighted hotspots

quiz.result_achievement_missed: Score at least 3/5 to earn a unit.

achievements.title: ACHIEVEMENTS
achievements.prize_bronze_desc: Your first step into alpine engineering mastery — you've proven basic knowledge of a rack system!
achievements.prize_silver_desc: An experienced alpine railway enthusiast — three systems conquered! Half the mountain is behind you.
achievements.prize_gold_desc: The ultimate achievement — all five rack systems mastered! You are a true Swiss railway expert.

locher.name: Locher System
locher.explanationShort: The system developed by Eduard Locher was specially constructed for the Pilatus railway, the world's steepest rack railway (up to 48% gradient).
locher.steepDangerText: At this extreme gradient, there was a danger that the gear wheels of conventional systems would jump off the rack due to enormous pressure.
locher.solutionText: Locher's ingenious solution: Two horizontally rotating gear wheels that engage laterally into a rack with teeth on both sides. Guide rollers below the rack lock the vehicle securely to the track.
locher.historyAndPurpose: Eduard Locher was a renowned Zurich engineer. The Pilatus railway was considered absolutely impossible to build at the time. Conventional systems like Riggenbach or Abt failed at the extreme slope: vertical gear wheels would have simply jumped over the teeth at 48%. Locher's unconventional horizontal idea revolutionised alpine rail transport.

hotspots.zahnstange_desc: A flat steel rail with right-angled teeth on both sides. Since it is laid in the middle, it enables the use of two symmetrically arranged gearboxes.
hotspots.treibraeder_desc: Two gear wheels rotate flat over the rails and engage from the sides. This mechanically cancels out the lateral thrust forces and prevents climbing.
hotspots.fuehrung_desc: Lower flange rollers physically grip under the head of the Locher rack. Even in extreme gusts of wind, lifting or derailment of the locomotive is impossible.

abt.name: Abt System
abt.explanationShort: System invented by Roman Abt, using two or three offset, narrow rack plates. It runs extremely smoothly and provides continuous tooth contact.
abt.steepDangerText: On simple racks in steep sections, the gear wheel tended to climb onto the rack and then re-engage. The consequences were major damage to both the rack and gear wheel.
abt.solutionText: Abt's ingenious answer: Offset thinner lamellae. While one gear wheel is just leaving a gap, the second gear wheel, offset slightly, is already fully engaging in the adjacent lamella.
abt.historyAndPurpose: Roman Abt was a student of Riggenbach and learned his trade in his workshops in Olten. He designed the offset lamellae to prevent the dreaded climbing of the gear wheel. The system was used on many Swiss mountain railways and even on broad-gauge railways in South America. In Switzerland, only the two-lamella design is used. The Brienz Rothorn Railway is the only line to reach the maximum gradient of 250 ‰ on average.

hotspots.lamellen_desc: Two (or three) thin, vertically standing tooth plates are mounted in parallel. Their teeth are arranged offset by half a tooth pitch.
hotspots.geteiltes_zahnrad_desc: The locomotive's drive gear wheel also consists of two physically offset gear rings that precisely match the offset rail lamellae.
hotspots.laufruhe_desc: Due to the offset, at every millisecond of travel, at least one drive tooth is fully loaded in engagement. This eliminates vibrations almost completely.

strub.name: Strub System
strub.explanationShort: Emil Strub developed this minimalist and highly economical single-head system, which is simpler to ballast and clean.
strub.steepDangerText: Complicated rack ladder constructions tend to become completely snowbound or iced over in icy winters, making journeys highly dangerous.
strub.solutionText: Strub's simple approach: The teeth are milled directly into the wide, solid head of a rolled broad-foot rail. On the sides, the rail head has a conical profile for safety clamps.
strub.historyAndPurpose: Emil Strub focused on economic efficiency and high-altitude suitability. The system celebrated its premiere on the legendary Jungfrau Railway. Since the snow can be easily swept away due to the open surface design, it is ideal for year-round tourism.

hotspots.konischer_kopf_desc: A solid, wedge-shaped rail profile. The teeth are thermally precision-milled on top, while the flanks remain smooth for mechanical clamp brakes.
hotspots.einfache_verlegung_desc: The Strub rail is wedged onto conventional steel sleepers. It requires fewer individual parts than all other historical predecessors.
hotspots.sicherheit_desc: Safety emergency clamps of the carriages grip around the conical rail head in case of danger and wedge themselves bomb-proof, preventing tipping.

riggenbach.name: Riggenbach System
riggenbach.explanationShort: The oldest European system, invented by Niklaus Riggenbach. It resembles a ladder where the teeth consist of round metal bolts.
riggenbach.steepDangerText: Early rail tracks were subjected to extreme bending forces and racks broke or bent easily under the ton-heavy weight of steam locomotives.
riggenbach.solutionText: Riggenbach's ingenious invention: Two U-iron profiles are riveted together at a constant distance with high-strength, round steel bars (rungs). A robust, durable lattice bed is created.
riggenbach.historyAndPurpose: Niklaus Riggenbach, the brilliant operations manager of the Centralbahn in Basel, had this technique patented when he wanted to conquer Mount Rigi. In 1871, the first mountain railway in Europe ran there. It was the absolute milestone of Swiss railway history.

hotspots.U_profile_desc: Two thick, hot-rolled C-profiles hold the construction together and support the sleeper attachment against lateral forces.
hotspots.sprossen_desc: Solid forged round steel bolts, precisely fitted horizontally at the tooth spacing. The locomotive gear wheel rolls extremely smoothly on these round pins.
hotspots.durchlass_desc: Since the ladder is hollow at the bottom, loose pebbles and sticky mud simply fall through the tooth bed. This prevents tooth jams.

von_roll.name: Von Roll System
von_roll.explanationShort: The Von Roll lamella rack – also called the Lamella system – consists of a single flat steel blade with vertical teeth. Developed as a complement and replacement for the Riggenbach and Strub rack, it is today the most widely used rack type in the world.
von_roll.steepDangerText: The rolled head-profile rails used in the Strub system eventually became unavailable. At the same time, switches for Riggenbach and Strub racks were very complex and expensive to build.
von_roll.solutionText: Von Roll's answer: a simple flat steel blade with machined teeth – similar in shape to the Abt system but wider. Switches are far simpler to build with this profile. Compatible with Riggenbach and Strub locomotives.
von_roll.historyAndPurpose: The Von Roll company has its roots in 1803 and grew into one of Switzerland's most important industrial groups. The lamella rack named after the company was never intended as a standalone system – rather as a complement to Riggenbach and Strub, primarily to simplify the complex switches. Today, virtually all new rack rails are built on the lamella principle. Railways equipped with Riggenbach or Strub racks already use lamella-style elements in their switches.

hotspots.monoblock_desc: Instead of a ladder or wedge rail: a single flat steel blade with vertically machined teeth. Wider than a single Abt lamella, simpler to manufacture than a Riggenbach ladder.
hotspots.abt_kompatibel_desc: Locomotives built for Riggenbach or Strub racks can run on the lamella rail – as long as Strub safety jaws are not in use. This allows gradual track renewals.
hotspots.lange_lebensdauer_desc: The key breakthrough of the Von Roll system: the flat profile is far easier to install in switch areas than the complex Riggenbach ladder. Almost all new rack railway switches are now built on this principle.

q1.question: What is the maximum gradient the Locher system can overcome?
q1.explanation: The Locher system on the Pilatus Railway holds the world record at 480 ‰ (48%) – the steepest rack railway on Earth!

q2.question: How do the gear wheels engage in the Locher system?
q2.explanation: Two horizontally mounted gear wheels engage from left and right into the central rack, preventing the gear from ever jumping out.

q3.question: What is the gauge (track width) of the Pilatus Railway?
q3.explanation: The Pilatus Railway uses an 800 mm gauge – narrower than standard to navigate the extremely steep and winding mountain terrain.

q4.question: What safety feature prevents the locomotive from lifting off the track?
options: Guide rollers gripping under the rack
Steel cables anchored to the mountain
q4.explanation: Lower flange guide rollers grip physically under the rack head, making it impossible for the vehicle to lift off even in extreme wind gusts.

q5.question: Who invented the Locher system?
q5.explanation: Eduard Locher, a renowned Zurich engineer, invented this revolutionary horizontal rack system specifically for the Pilatus Railway in 1889.

q1.question: Why did Roman Abt develop offset lamella plates?
options: To save weight in the boiler
So that a tooth is always fully engaged for smooth running
q1.explanation: The offset ensures that gear wheel transitions happen continuously without jerky idle strokes – a milestone in smooth mountain railway operation.

q2.question: Who was Roman Abt's teacher in railway engineering?
q2.explanation: Roman Abt was a student of Riggenbach and learned his trade in Riggenbach's workshops in Olten before developing his own improved system.

q3.question: How many lamella plates does the Swiss variant of the Abt system use?
q3.explanation: In Switzerland, exclusively the two-lamella design is used, though three-lamella variants exist elsewhere in the world.

q4.question: Which famous Swiss railway uses the Abt system at maximum gradient?
q4.explanation: The Brienz Rothorn Railway is the only line to reach the Abt system's maximum gradient of 250 ‰ on average.

q5.question: What problem does the Abt offset design solve?
options: Gear wheel climbing off the rack
q5.explanation: The dreaded 'climbing' problem — where the gear wheel would jump onto the rack and cause major damage — is eliminated by the offset lamellae ensuring continuous engagement.

q1.question: What makes the Strub system ideal for winter operation?
options: Open surface design allows easy snow removal
q1.explanation: The Strub system's open surface design means snow can be easily swept away, making it ideal for year-round tourism even in harsh alpine winters.

q2.question: Which famous railway premiered the Strub system?
q2.explanation: The Strub system celebrated its premiere on the legendary Jungfrau Railway, one of the most iconic mountain railways in the world.

q3.question: How are the teeth created in the Strub system?
options: Milled directly into the solid rail head
q3.explanation: The teeth are milled directly into the wide, massive head of a rolled broad-foot rail — a minimalist and economical single-piece design.

q4.question: What safety feature uses the conical sides of the Strub rail head?
options: Emergency safety clamps that wedge around the head
q4.explanation: Safety emergency clamps grip around the conical rail head in case of danger, wedging themselves bomb-proof to prevent the carriage from tipping.

q5.question: What was Emil Strub's main design priority?
q5.explanation: Emil Strub focused on economic efficiency and high-altitude suitability, creating the most minimalist and cost-effective rack system of its era.

q1.question: Which rack system resembles a ladder with round bolt rungs?
q1.explanation: Riggenbach's system uses an open steel bed with round rung bolts between two U-profiles — giving it the distinctive ladder-like appearance.

q2.question: In which year did the first European mountain railway operate?
q2.explanation: In 1871, the Rigi Railway — using Riggenbach's system — became the first mountain railway in Europe, a milestone in Swiss railway history.

q3.question: What advantage does the hollow ladder design provide?
q3.explanation: Since the ladder is hollow at the bottom, loose pebbles and sticky mud simply fall through the tooth bed, preventing tooth jams — a self-cleaning design.

q4.question: What was Niklaus Riggenbach's role at the Centralbahn?
q4.explanation: Niklaus Riggenbach was the brilliant Operations Manager of the Centralbahn in Basel when he patented his rack railway technique to conquer Mount Rigi.

q5.question: What material were the original Riggenbach rungs made from?
q5.explanation: The rungs were solid forged round steel bolts (originally wrought iron), precisely fitted horizontally at the tooth spacing for smooth gear engagement.

q1.question: For what purpose was the Von Roll lamella rack originally developed?
options: As a replacement for the Locher system
As a complement to simplify Riggenbach and Strub switches
As a completely independent new system
For broad-gauge railways in South America
q1.explanation: The lamella rack was never planned as a standalone system, but as a complement – above all to simplify the complex switches required by Riggenbach and Strub racks.

q2.question: With which systems is the Von Roll lamella rack compatible?
q2.explanation: Vehicles for Riggenbach and Strub lines can run on the lamella rail – as long as Strub safety jaws are not used. Perfect for gradual track modernisation.

q3.question: What does a Von Roll lamella rack look like?
options: Like a ladder with round bolts
A single flat steel blade with vertical teeth
A hollow tube with external teeth
q3.explanation: The lamella rack is a single flat steel blade – similar to the Abt system but wider. No ladder structure, no wedge rail.

q4.question: When were the foundations of the Von Roll company established?
q4.explanation: The Von Roll company has its roots in 1803, growing into one of Switzerland's most important industrial companies.

q5.question: Where is the Von Roll lamella principle most commonly found today?
options: On narrow-gauge lines in South America
In the switches of railways with Riggenbach or Strub racks
q5.explanation: The lamella principle has become dominant particularly in switch construction. Today, almost all new rack railway switches are built on this principle.

locher.explanationShort: This is the coolest train system ever! It goes up the super steep Pilatus mountain – almost like climbing a wall! 🏔️
locher.steepDangerText: The mountain is SO steep that normal train wheels would just slip off the rails. Imagine trying to climb a really steep slide!
locher.solutionText: The clever trick: Instead of wheels on top, this train has sideways wheels that grab the rail from both sides – like your hands grabbing a pole! It can never fall off! 🎉

abt.explanationShort: This smart system uses two tooth-bars side by side, but slightly shifted. This way, the train always has a good grip! 💪
abt.steepDangerText: On old single rail systems, the gear wheel sometimes jumped up and damaged things. That was really dangerous!
abt.solutionText: Roman Abt's smart idea: Use TWO toothed bars next to each other, shifted a tiny bit. When one wheel leaves a gap, the other is already firmly gripping! 🧩

strub.explanationShort: This system is super simple and works great in winter snow! The teeth are carved right into one strong rail. ❄️
strub.steepDangerText: In winter, complicated rail systems get filled with snow and ice. The train can't move safely anymore! ⛄
strub.solutionText: Emil Strub made it simple: just one strong rail with teeth cut into the top. Snow slides right off! Easy to clean, easy to fix! ☀️

riggenbach.explanationShort: This is the oldest mountain railway system in Europe! It looks like a ladder lying on the ground with round metal bars. 🪜
riggenbach.steepDangerText: Very early trains were super heavy and the tracks would bend and break under their weight!
riggenbach.solutionText: Riggenbach's great idea: Build a strong ladder from two metal bars with round rungs between them. The train's gear wheel rolls on these rungs like climbing a ladder! 🪜

von_roll.explanationShort: This system is made from a single flat steel blade – simple and clever! It was invented to make switches on rack railways much easier. ⚙️
von_roll.steepDangerText: The old rack rails stopped being made and the switches were very complicated and expensive to build!
von_roll.solutionText: Von Roll's solution: a simple flat steel blade with teeth – no ladder, no clamp system. The switches are much simpler! 🔧

q1.question: How steep can the Locher train climb?
q1.explanation: The Locher train climbs Mount Pilatus at 48% – that's almost like going up a wall! 🏔️

q2.question: What makes the Locher system special?
options: Its wheels grab the rail from the sides
q2.explanation: The Locher train has sideways wheels that grab the rail from both sides – like your hands holding a pole! 🤲

q3.question: How wide are the Locher train tracks?
q3.explanation: The tracks are 800 mm wide – narrower than normal so the train can fit on the steep mountain! 🚂

q4.question: What stops the train from flying off in strong wind?
options: Special rollers that grab underneath the rail
q4.explanation: Special rollers grab under the rail so the train can never lift off – even in super windy weather! 💨

q5.question: Who invented this amazing train system?
q5.explanation: Eduard Locher was a super smart engineer from Zurich who invented this in 1889! 🧠

q1.question: Why does the Abt system use TWO toothed bars?
options: Because one is for going up and one for going down
So the train always has a firm grip and rides smoothly
Because it looks cooler that way
q1.explanation: Two bars shifted a little bit means the train always has at least one wheel firmly gripping. No bumps, no slipping! 🧩

q2.question: Who taught Roman Abt about trains?
q2.explanation: Roman Abt learned from Niklaus Riggenbach in his workshop – then made his own even better system! 📚

q3.question: What was the BIG problem with old single-bar systems?
options: The gear wheel would jump up and break things!
q3.explanation: The gear wheel would sometimes jump up off the rail – very dangerous! Abt's two-bar system fixed that forever! ✅

q4.question: Which mountain train uses the Abt system?
q4.explanation: The Brienz Rothorn Railway uses the Abt system to climb up to 250 ‰ steepness! 🏔️

q5.question: How does the Abt system make the ride smooth?
options: One wheel always has a firm grip on the teeth!
q5.explanation: Because the bars are shifted, one wheel is always perfectly gripping – so no bumping! Smooth like butter! 🧈

q1.question: Why is the Strub system great in winter?
options: Snow slides off the simple open design!
q1.explanation: The Strub rail is so simple and open that snow just slides right off! Perfect for snowy mountains! ❄️

q2.question: Which famous railway uses the Strub system?
q2.explanation: The super famous Jungfrau Railway in Switzerland uses Strub's clever system! 🏔️

q3.question: How are the teeth made on a Strub rail?
options: Carved directly into one strong rail!
q3.explanation: The teeth are carved right into the top of one big, strong rail – simple and tough! 💪

q4.question: What happens in an emergency on a Strub train?
options: Special clamps grab the rail super tight!
q4.explanation: Emergency clamps grab around the rail head and hold on super tight – the train can never tip over! 🦾

q5.question: What was Emil Strub's big idea?
options: Keep it simple and make it work in the mountains!
Make it as complicated as possible
q5.explanation: Emil Strub wanted to keep things simple and tough – perfect for high mountain railways! 🎯

q1.question: What does the Riggenbach system look like?
options: A ladder lying on the ground! 🪜
q1.explanation: Riggenbach's system has round metal bars between two rails – just like the rungs of a ladder! 🪜

q2.question: When did the first mountain train in Europe start running?
q2.explanation: In 1871, the Rigi Railway became the very FIRST mountain railway in all of Europe! 🎉

q3.question: What cool thing can the ladder design do?
options: Rocks and mud fall through it – self-cleaning!
q3.explanation: Because the ladder is hollow at the bottom, stones and mud fall right through – it cleans itself! 🧹

q4.question: Where did Riggenbach work?
q4.explanation: Niklaus Riggenbach was the clever boss at the Centralbahn railway company in Basel! 🚂

q5.question: What are the rungs made of?
q5.explanation: The rungs are made from super strong forged iron and steel – they can hold up really heavy trains! 💪

q1.question: What was the Von Roll lamella system built to do?
options: Make switches on rack railways simpler!
q1.explanation: The Von Roll system makes switches on rack railways much simpler! ⚙️

q2.question: Which old systems can work with the lamella rack?
q2.explanation: Trains for Riggenbach and Strub lines can run on the lamella rail! 🚂

q3.question: What does a lamella rack look like?
options: Like a single flat steel blade!
q3.explanation: The lamella rack is a simple flat steel blade – no complicated structure! 💪

q4.question: When was the Von Roll company founded?
q4.explanation: The Von Roll company has existed since 1803 – that's very old! 🏭

q5.question: Where do you see the Von Roll principle most today?
options: In the switches of almost all rack railways!
q5.explanation: Almost all new switches on rack railways today use the Von Roll lamella principle! ⚙️
